beats.text: Here's a square matrix A — watch what it does to vectors in the plane.
Most vectors rotate and stretch — they tip off their original direction. But some special vectors stay locked on their line. The matrix just scales them. Those are the eigenvectors.
Take a projection matrix. An arrow along the projection line holds perfectly still — eigenvalue one. An arrow pointing perpendicular gets crushed flat to the origin — eigenvalue zero.

Now a permutation matrix that swaps two coordinates. The diagonal arrow at one, one bounces straight back — eigenvalue one. The arrow at one, negative-one flips to negative-one, one — eigenvalue negative one.
To find eigenvalues, solve det of A minus lambda-I equals zero. A highlight surrounds the diagonal entries as lambda gets subtracted from each. The determinant equation drops down below.
Sanity check: the eigenvalues must sum to the trace and multiply to the determinant of A. Two instant checks — no extra work.
